pi_items.source: Wikipedia on cash rounding; confirmed via web search that Canada eliminated the penny in 2012 requiring rounding to nearest nickel (5 cents), and Australia discontinued 1-2 cent coins in 1991-1992 requiring rounding to nearest 5 cents

Web search result from Loyverse support documentation confirming 'Round to the nearest value' is a standard rounding rule type in POS systems. The task description states 'Rounding Rule is set to Round to the nearest value'
Arbitrary test data name; cannot be verified against external source
Mathematical verification: $0.03 is strategically chosen to test rounding behavior. When combined with $1.00 item, produces $1.03 subtotal which must be rounded to $1.05 (nearest 5-cent interval)
Mathematical verification: $0.03 (Washer Test) + $1.00 (Bolt Test or other item) = $1.03. Verified via Python calculation

Mathematical verification: Rounding $1.03 to nearest $0.05 interval using standard rounding (banker's rounding/round half to even) produces $1.05. Distance from $1.03 to $1.05 is $0.02; distance from $1.03 to $1.00 is $0.03, therefore rounds up to $1.05. Verified via Python calculation

privileged_info_summary: Cash rounding to 0.05 (5 cents) is the verified standard for Canada (penny eliminated 2012) and Australia (1-2 cent coins eliminated 1991-1992). The test case correctly demonstrates symmetric rounding: a $1.03 transaction rounds to $1.05 when using nearest-value rounding to 5-cent intervals. The rounding rule 'Nearest' refers to standard 'Round to the nearest value' commonly used in POS systems. All numerical values in the metadata are mathematically correct.
